lead: Living in Newmarket means making a home in the global headquarters of horse racing, a CB8 town poised on the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border. It is a place of two halves in the best sense: a working market town with shops, schools and a real community, set against the open sweep of the gallops and the studs that surround it. Since 2005 our family-run crews have moved people into every corner of the town. Here is our guide to Newmarket's areas and amenities.

sections.h: The Town Centre and High Street
sections.body:
The heart of Newmarket is its High Street, and for many residents it is the main reason they love the town. It covers most everyday needs, from grocers and bakers to the kind of independent shops that give a place its character. Living close to the centre means you can do the bulk of your shopping on foot, which is a genuine pleasure in a town this compact and a real selling point for anyone tired of relying on the car for every errand.

The central streets themselves are a mix of handsome older terraces and town houses, many with histories tied to the racing trade that has shaped Newmarket for generations. They suit people who like to be in the thick of things, within strolling distance of the shops, the cafes and the comings and goings of town life. There is a sociable, lived-in feel to these streets that newcomers warm to quickly.

The trade-off, as we always point out when helping people move in, is space and parking. The older central roads can be narrow, and on-street parking is at a premium. If a driveway and a generous garden matter more to you than a short walk to the shops, the centre may not be your spot. But for the convenience and the character, plenty of residents would not live anywhere else.

Move out from the centre and Newmarket opens up into newer developments and quieter residential roads. Here the housing tends towards more modern family homes, often with off-street parking, a proper garden and a little more breathing room all round. These areas are popular with growing families who want the space to spread out without giving up the convenience of having the town close at hand.

What strikes most people about these edges of Newmarket is how quickly the green setting takes over. The studs and training establishments dominate the surrounding countryside, so much of the land around the town is given to horses rather than houses. That keeps the outlook open and rural even quite close to home, which is a rare combination and one residents value highly once they have it.

From a moving point of view, these newer areas are usually the most straightforward to work in. The roads are wider, parking is easier, and access for a removals lorry rarely causes a problem. When we survey a move to one of these streets, the day tends to be smooth and predictable, which is exactly what you want when you are settling into a new family home.

Newmarket works well for families, with schooling and the usual local services within easy reach. One quirk worth understanding early is the town's border position: with Newmarket straddling Suffolk and Cambridgeshire, school catchments and admissions can depend on which authority covers your address. We always flag this to families moving in, because sorting it out at the start is far easier than untangling it after the fact.

Beyond the practicalities, there is a strong sense of community here. The racing industry gives the town a shared identity that few places can match, and the calendar of the racing year shapes its busiest and quietest spells. Whether or not you have any connection to the horses, you soon find yourself swept up in the rhythm of the town, and that shared culture makes it easier to feel at home.

Everyday amenities are well catered for, with the High Street covering daily needs and larger shopping and services within easy reach by car. Families tell us they appreciate that Newmarket never feels cut off despite being a smaller town, which can be a real worry when leaving a larger place. The balance of community feel and practical convenience is what keeps people here for the long term.

No guide to living in Newmarket would be complete without the gallops and the studs, because they shape daily life more than anything else. The training grounds are active from before dawn, and racehorses have right of way across the town's dedicated crossings. The sight of a long string of thoroughbreds crossing the road in the early light is something residents never quite take for granted, and it is a large part of what makes the place special.

For anyone working in racing, Newmarket is the natural home, and a great many of the people we move here are relocating for jobs at the studs, the yards or the Jockey Club at the town's heart. The whole town is geared to support that world, with specialist services and suppliers close at hand. Even those with no link to the industry find the surrounding studs give the area a calm, purposeful character all of its own.

If you settle very close to the main training yards, do bear in mind the early-morning activity. Light sleepers sometimes find the dawn bustle takes a little getting used to, though most people come to see it as part of the charm. When we help people choose where to settle, we are always happy to share what we have learned over the years about which parts of town suit which sort of lifestyle.

Newmarket's location on the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border makes it a practical base for getting about. Much of daily life is walkable, especially near the centre, and the town is compact enough that you are rarely far from what you need. For longer trips the road links are good, connecting the town out towards the wider region and the city of Cambridge, which is close enough to make commuting genuinely workable.

That proximity to Cambridge is a big draw, opening up a wide range of work without having to live in the city itself. Many residents enjoy the best of both worlds: the space and character of a Suffolk-border town during their evenings and weekends, and easy access to the city for work during the week. The train connection adds further options for those who would rather not drive.

For visitors and family further afield, the road and rail links keep Newmarket well connected, so settling here never means cutting yourself off from the people you care about. When we move people into the town, we often hear afterwards how pleasantly surprised they are by how easy it is to get around. Spend a little time learning your new routes in the first week and the town quickly starts to feel like home.

sections.h: Why People Choose to Stay in Newmarket
sections.body:
Plenty of towns are pleasant enough to pass through, but Newmarket is the kind of place where people tend to put down roots and stay. Part of that is the heritage and the racing culture, which give the town an identity you do not find everywhere and a sense of belonging that builds over time. Once you have lived alongside the gallops for a season or two, the daily sight of the horses becomes something you would miss terribly if you ever left.

Another part is the balance the town strikes. It is small enough to feel friendly and knowable, yet well enough connected, thanks to its border position and the closeness of Cambridge, that you never feel cut off from work, services or the wider world. That combination of community feel and practical convenience is genuinely hard to find, and it is what brings so many of the people we move here back to us when they next need a hand.

For families especially, Newmarket offers a settled, rooted kind of life. Children grow up surrounded by the town's distinctive culture, the open countryside of the studs is always close, and the amenities for everyday living are comfortably within reach. When people ask us whether Newmarket is a good place to settle for the long term, our honest answer, drawn from years of moving people in and very rarely out, is a warm and unhesitating yes.

faqs: Which areas of Newmarket are best for families?

The quieter residential edges of Newmarket suit families best, with newer homes, off-street parking and gardens, plus the open countryside of the surrounding studs close by. Just check which council covers your address, as the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border affects school catchments.
Is it easy to shop and get around in Newmarket?

Yes, especially near the centre. Newmarket's High Street covers most everyday needs on foot, the town is compact and walkable, and good road and rail links connect you to Cambridge and the wider region for work and longer trips.
What is the community like in Newmarket?

Newmarket has a strong, shared identity built around its racing heritage. The calendar of the racing year shapes the town's rhythm, and whether or not you work with the horses, that common culture makes it easy to feel part of the community.
Does living near the gallops in Newmarket cause any issues?

The main thing to know is the early-morning activity around the training yards, which light sleepers sometimes take time to adjust to. Most residents grow to love the dawn sight of racehorses on the gallops, but it is worth considering how close you settle.
